epigraph.text:
He determines the number of the stars and calls them each 
by name.

content.text:
A child on a Ferris wheel rises, crests, descends, and rises again. 
Ocean tides swell and recede with the moon. A guitar string vibrates 
back and forth hundreds of times each second. These phenomena share a 
common thread: they *repeat*. The mathematics of repetition is the 
mathematics of trigonometric functions, and you have been studying them 
since your first encounter with right triangles.

This section is a *review*. You should already know the definitions 
of sine, cosine, and tangent; you should be comfortable evaluating them at 
standard angles and sketching their graphs. Our purpose here is not to 
teach these ideas from scratch but to *sharpen the tools* we will need 
throughout calculus. When we differentiate $\sin x$ in Chapter 4, when we 
integrate $\sec^{2} x$ in Chapter 6, when we tackle trigonometric 
substitution in Chapter 8—we will need these facts at our fingertips, 
not buried under dust.
Let us begin where all of trigonometry begins: the unit circle.
The Unit Circle

The unit circle is the circle of radius $1$ centered at the origin in the 
$xy$-plane. Every point on this circle can be reached by starting at 
$(1,0)$ and traveling a signed arc length $t$: counterclockwise when $t > 0$, 
clockwise when $t < 0$. The terminal point of that arc is the key to 
everything.

content.title: Sine and Cosine
content.content:
Let $t$ be a real number, and let $P(x,y)$ be the point on the unit circle 
reached by traveling an arc of length $\abs{t}$ from $(1,0)$, 
counterclockwise if $t > 0$ and clockwise if $t < 0$. Then
\[
 \cos t = x, \qquad \sin t = y.
\]
The **cosine** of $t$ is the $x$-coordinate, and the 
**sine** of $t$ is the $y$-coordinate, of the terminal 
point $P$.

content.text:
Because $P$ lies on the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$, we obtain the most 
fundamental identity in all of trigonometry for free:
$$\cos^{2} t + \sin^{2} t = 1 \quad \text{for every real number } t.$$

The remaining four trigonometric functions are defined in terms of sine and 
cosine:

content.title: The Four Remaining Trigonometric Functions
content.content:
\[
 \tan t = \frac{\sin t}{\cos t}, \qquad
 \cot t = \frac{\cos t}{\sin t}, \qquad
 \sec t = \frac{1}{\cos t}, \qquad
 \csc t = \frac{1}{\sin t},
\]
each defined wherever its denominator is nonzero.

content.text: Key Values: The Reference Triangles

Two special right triangles—the **45–45–90** and the 
**30–60–90**—produce every "nice" value of sine and cosine that 
you are expected to know. When these triangles are inscribed in the unit 
circle, the hypotenuse has length $1$, and the legs give the coordinates of 
the terminal point.

The table below collects the values you should know cold. Do not memorize 
it mechanically—learn the two reference triangles, and you can reconstruct 
every entry.

content.title: Evaluating Trig at Any Standard Angle
content.content:
To find $\sin t$ or $\cos t$ at any standard angle:

1. **Find the reference angle.** The reference angle 
 $\bar{t}$ is the acute angle between the terminal side and the 
 $x$-axis.

2. **Evaluate at the reference angle.** Use the table above 
 (or the reference triangle) to find $\sin\bar{t}$ and $\cos\bar{t}$.

3. **Attach the correct sign.** Determine which quadrant the 
 terminal side lies in, and apply the appropriate sign 
 (All Students Take Calculus: all positive in QI, sine in QII, 
 tangent in QIII, cosine in QIV).

content.title: Evaluating at a non-first-quadrant angle
content.solution:
The angle $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ lies in the second quadrant. Its reference 
angle is
\[
 \bar{t} = \pi - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}.
\]
From the table, $\sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$ and 
$\cos\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In the second quadrant, sine is 
positive and cosine is negative, so
\[
 \sin\frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}, \qquad 
 \cos\frac{5\pi}{6} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.\qedhere
\]

content.title: A third-quadrant angle
content.solution:
The angle $\frac{4\pi}{3}$ lies in the third quadrant. The reference 
angle is
\[
 \bar{t} = \frac{4\pi}{3} - \pi = \frac{\pi}{3}.
\]
We know $\tan\frac{\pi}{3} = \sqrt{3}$. In the third quadrant, tangent 
is positive (both sine and cosine are negative, so their ratio is 
positive). Therefore,
\[
 \tan\frac{4\pi}{3} = \sqrt{3}.\qedhere
\]

content.text: Graphs of the Trigonometric Functions

The graphs of $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ are the fundamental waveforms of 
mathematics. Both are continuous, smooth, and periodic with 
period $2\pi$.

content.title: Period and Amplitude
content.content:
A function $f$ is **periodic** with 
**period** $T > 0$ if
\[
 f(x + T) = f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \text{ in the domain of } f,
\]
and $T$ is the smallest such positive number. For a sinusoidal function 
$f(x) = A\sin(Bx + C) + D$ or $f(x) = A\cos(Bx + C) + D$, the 
**amplitude** is $\abs{A}$, the period is 
$\dfrac{2\pi}{\abs{B}}$, the **phase shift** is 
$-\dfrac{C}{B}$, and the **vertical shift** is $D$.

content.text: Key features to remember:

items:
**Symmetry:**\ $\sin x$ is an *odd* function 
 ($\sin(-x) = -\sin x$), and $\cos x$ is an *even* function 
 ($\cos(-x) = \cos x$).

content.title: Reading a transformed sinusoid
content.problem:
Identify the amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift of
\[
 f(x) = 3\sin\!\left(2x - \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 1.
\]
content.solution:
We compare with the general form $A\sin(Bx + C) + D$. Here $A = 3$, 
$B = 2$, $C = -\frac{\pi}{3}$, and $D = 1$. Therefore:

- Amplitude $= \abs{A} = 3$.

- Period $= \dfrac{2\pi}{\abs{B}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$.

- Phase shift $= -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{-\pi/3}{2} 
 = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ (shifted $\frac{\pi}{6}$ to the right).

- Vertical shift $= D = 1$ (shifted $1$ unit up).
The graph oscillates between $1 - 3 = -2$ and $1 + 3 = 4$, completing 
one full cycle every $\pi$ units.

content.text: The Tangent Function and Its Relatives

The graph of $y = \tan x$ looks quite different from the gentle waves of 
sine and cosine. Because $\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$, vertical 
asymptotes occur wherever $\cos x = 0$—that is, at 
$x = \pm\frac{\pi}{2}, \pm\frac{3\pi}{2}, \ldots$ Between consecutive 
asymptotes the function increases from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, giving 
tangent a period of $\pi$ (not $2\pi$).

The reciprocal functions—$\csc x = 1/\sin x$, $\sec x = 1/\cos x$, 
and $\cot x = \cos x / \sin x$—arise less often in elementary calculus 
but will appear when we study certain integrals and derivatives. Their 
graphs inherit asymptotes from the zeros of sine and cosine, respectively.

content.title: Tangent is not bounded
content.content:
Unlike sine and cosine, the tangent function has no finite maximum or 
minimum. Its range is $(-\infty,\infty)$. Do not assume that all 
trigonometric functions are bounded—this is a common source of errors 
in limit problems.

content.text: Key Identities: A Reference

The trigonometric identities below are tools, not theorems to be proved 
from scratch every time you need them. You proved them in precalculus; 
now you should *know* them. We collect them here for reference, 
organized by type.
*Pythagorean Identities.**

(The second and third follow by dividing eq:pyth1 by $\cos^{2} t$ 
and $\sin^{2} t$, respectively.)
*Sum and Difference Formulas.**

$$\sin(\alpha \pm \beta) &= \sin\alpha\cos\beta 
    \pm \cos\alpha\sin\beta, \\[4pt]
  \cos(\alpha \pm \beta) &= \cos\alpha\cos\beta 
    \mp \sin\alpha\sin\beta, \\[4pt]
  \tan(\alpha \pm \beta) &= \frac{\tan\alpha \pm \tan\beta}
    {1 \mp \tan\alpha\tan\beta}.$$
*Double-Angle Formulas.**

$$\sin 2\theta &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta, \\[4pt]
  \cos 2\theta &= \cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta 
    = 2\cos^{2}\theta - 1 = 1 - 2\sin^{2}\theta.$$
*Half-Angle (Power-Reduction) Formulas.**%

$$\sin^{2}\theta &= \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}, 
    \\[4pt]
  \cos^{2}\theta &= \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}.$$

These will be indispensable in Chapter 8 when we integrate even powers 
of sine and cosine.

content.title: Using identities to simplify
content.solution:
We apply the double-angle formulas. The numerator is 
$\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$. For the denominator, we use the form 
$\cos 2x = 2\cos^{2} x - 1$, so
\[
 1 + \cos 2x = 1 + (2\cos^{2} x - 1) = 2\cos^{2} x.
\]
Therefore,
\[
 \frac{\sin 2x}{1 + \cos 2x} = \frac{2\sin x\cos x}{2\cos^{2} x} 
 = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x} = \tan x,
\]
provided $\cos x \neq 0$.

content.title: Verifying an identity
content.solution:
Starting from the left side and applying eq:pyth2 rearranged:
\[
 \sec^{2}\theta - 1 = (1 + \tan^{2}\theta) - 1 = \tan^{2}\theta.
\]
The two sides are equal for all $\theta$ where both are defined.

content.text: Inverse Trigonometric Functions: A Preview

None of the six trigonometric functions is one-to-one on its natural 
domain—each repeats its values infinitely often. To define an inverse, 
we must *restrict* the domain to an interval on which the function is 
one-to-one.

content.title: Principal Inverse Trigonometric Functions
content.content:
1. $y = \sin^{-1} x$ (or $\arcsin x$) is defined for $x \in [-1,1]$ 
 with range $\left[-\frac{\pi}{2},\, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$. It returns 
 the unique angle in this interval whose sine is $x$.

2. $y = \cos^{-1} x$ (or $\arccos x$) is defined for $x \in [-1,1]$ 
 with range $[0,\pi]$. It returns the unique angle in this interval 
 whose cosine is $x$.

3. $y = \tan^{-1} x$ (or $\arctan x$) is defined for 
 $x \in (-\infty,\infty)$ with range 
 $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2},\, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. It returns the unique 
 angle in this interval whose tangent is $x$.

content.title: $\sin^{-1} x$ does not mean $(\sin x)^{-1}$
content.content:
The notation $\sin^{-1} x$ denotes the *inverse function* of sine, 
not the reciprocal. We write $(\sin x)^{-1} = \csc x$ for the 
reciprocal. This is an unfortunate but standard ambiguity. When in doubt, 
use the notation $\arcsin x$.

content.title: Evaluating inverse trig functions
content.solution:
1. We need an angle $\theta \in [-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}]$ with 
 $\sin\theta = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. From our table, 
 $\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}$.

2. We need $\theta \in (-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2})$ with 
 $\tan\theta = -1$. Since $\tan(-\frac{\pi}{4}) = -1$, we have 
 $\theta = -\frac{\pi}{4}$.

content.text:
We will return to the inverse trigonometric functions in Chapter 7, where 
we study their derivatives and the elegant integrals they produce. For now, 
it is enough to know what they mean and how to evaluate them at standard 
values.
Trigonometric Functions in the World

Why do trigonometric functions appear so often in nature? Because nature 
is full of *cycles*. Anything that repeats—tides, seasons, 
heartbeats, vibrating strings, alternating current, the orbit of a 
planet—can be modeled by sinusoidal functions.

content.title: Modeling daylight hours
content.problem:
In Austin, Texas, the longest day (June 21) has about $14.0$ hours of 
daylight and the shortest day (December 21) has about $10.1$ hours. Write 
a sinusoidal model $D(t)$ for the hours of daylight $t$ days after 
January 1.
content.solution:
The average daylight is
\[
 D_{\text{avg}} = \frac{14.0 + 10.1}{2} = 12.05 \text{ hours}.
\]
The amplitude is
\[
 A = \frac{14.0 - 10.1}{2} = 1.95 \text{ hours}.
\]
The period is $365$ days, so $B = \frac{2\pi}{365}$. The maximum occurs 
around day $t = 172$ (June 21). Since cosine reaches its maximum at $0$, 
we shift:
\[
 D(t) = 12.05 + 1.95\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{365}(t - 172)\right).
\]
This model predicts $D(172) = 12.05 + 1.95 = 14.0$ hours at the summer 
solstice and $D(355) \approx 12.05 - 1.95 = 10.1$ hours at the winter 
solstice, matching the data.

content.title: Simple harmonic motion
content.problem:
A mass on a spring is pulled $6$\,cm below its rest position and released. 
It completes one full oscillation every $0.8$ seconds. Find a function 
$y(t)$ giving the displacement (in cm) from the rest position at time 
$t$ seconds.
content.solution:
Since the mass is released from below the rest position 
(displacement $= -6$ at $t = 0$), we use
\[
 y(t) = -A\cos(Bt).
\]
The amplitude is $A = 6$ and the period is $T = 0.8$, giving 
$B = \frac{2\pi}{0.8} = \frac{5\pi}{2}$. Therefore,
\[
 y(t) = -6\cos\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{2}\,t\right).
\]
We can verify: $y(0) = -6$ (starting position), 
$y(0.4) = -6\cos({\pi}) = 6$ (maximum displacement above rest), and 
$y(0.8) = -6\cos({2\pi}) = -6$ (back to starting position).

content.title: Jean Baptiste Joseph Fourier (1768--1830)
content.content:
Fourier shocked the mathematical world by claiming that *any* periodic 
function can be written as a sum of sines and cosines. His 
*Th\'eorie analytique de la chaleur* (1822) showed how these 
"Fourier series" solve problems in heat conduction, and the idea 
revolutionized physics and engineering. Today, Fourier analysis is 
essential to signal processing, medical imaging, and data compression. 
Every time you stream music or view a JPEG image, Fourier's insight is 
at work.

content.text: Looking Ahead

We have reviewed the definitions, graphs, identities, and applications of 
the trigonometric functions. These tools are not ornamental—they are 
load-bearing. In the chapters ahead, we will:

items:
Compute $\limto{x \to 0}\dfrac{\sin x}{x}$ and use it to 
 differentiate $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ from the definition (Chapter 3).

Develop the derivatives of all six trigonometric functions 
 (Chapter 4).

Use inverse trig functions to evaluate integrals such as 
 $\displaystyle\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}}\dx$ (Chapter 7).

Master trigonometric substitution and the integration of powers 
 of sine and cosine (Chapter 8).

content.text:
Every identity in this section will earn its keep. Make sure these tools 
are sharp; you are going to need them.

marginNotes.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies 
proclaim the work of his hands.

He is before all things, and in him all 
things hold together.

And God said, "Let there be lights in the 
vault of the sky to separate the day from the night, and let them serve as 
signs to mark sacred times, and days and years."

marginNotes.author: Joseph Fourier
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics compares the most diverse 
phenomena and discovers the secret analogies that unite them.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
For since the fabric of the universe is most 
perfect and the work of a most wise Creator, nothing at all takes place in 
the universe in which some rule of maximum or minimum does not appear.
